coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of non-modified near-infrared carbon dots for hypochlorite detection and cell membrane imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Lan-Fang
Fang, Wen-Le
Zhang, Bin
Xu, Qi-Jie
coredata.dc:description: Devising carbon dots with long wavelength emission (red light or near infrared), high selectivity and good bio-compatibility is critical in fluorescence detection and imaging, but achieving this goal remains a great challenge. Herein, near-infrared emissive carbon dots (NIR-CDs) with obvious emission characteristic of 653 nm were synthesized through hydrothermally treatment of toluidine bule and gallic acid. Noticeably, the NIR-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity to hypochlorite (ClO−), and the limit of detection is as low as 42.7 nM. The selective recognition reaction between ClO− and the surface functional groups of NIR-CDs inhibits the fluorescence from NIR-CDs. The quenching mechanism was confirmed by fluorescence lifetime decays, FT-IR spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectra. More remarkably, the NIR-CDs have rich hydrophilic groups showed lower cytotoxicity, excellent bio-compatibility and specific cell membrane localization ability. The established spectrofluorometric method based on NIR-CDs has been used to determination of ClO− level in tap water sample, the recoveries were 97.7 %–103.3 %. In addition, the NIR-CDs also has been successfully applied for the imaging of cell membrane. The study provides a novel idea for developing NIR ClO− probe as well as cell membrane localization probe based on CDs, which present bright prospects in real water samples monitoring and cell membrane imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared carbon dots
Nanomaterial
Hypochlorite
Fluorescence detection
Hydrothermal method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the NIR-CDs and the inset shows the HRTEM image of a single CD; (B) Particle size distribution of the NIR-CDs.
XRD patterns (A) and FT-IR spectrum (B) of the NIR-CDs.
(A) XPS full spectrum of the NIR-CDs. High resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C), and S2p (D).
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NIR-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NIR-CDs (15 μg·mL−1) under different excitation wavelengths from 540 to 650 nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NIR-CDs (15 μg·mL−1) after the addition of different concentrations of ClO− (0–25 μM); Inset: The fluorescent photographic images of NIR-CDs before and after the addition of ClO−. (B) The linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentrations of ClO− from 2 to 12 μM. (C, D) Fluorescence selectivity of the NIR-CDs for ClO− (25 μM) over other various relevant analytes (250 μM).

Confocal fluorescence images of NIR-CDs and ClO− in MCF-7 cells. (A, C) Bright field images; (B) Red channel images (λex = 561 nm) of MCF-7 cells incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 3 h; (D) Cells were pre-incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 3 h then incubated with ClO− (80 μM) for 20 min. Scale bar: 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cells incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 3 h, then further incubated with DiO (5 μM) for 25 min. (A) Confocal fluorescence images of NIR-CDs (Red, λex = 561 nm); (B) Confocal fluorescence images of DiO (Green, λex = 488 nm); (C) Overlay of (A) and (B); (D) Bright field images; (E) Intensity scatter plot of the NIR-CDs and DiO. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the preparation of the NIR-CDs and their application for the detection of ClO− and cell membrane imaging.
Comparison of different CD-based probe for ClO− detection, “/” means that it is not mentioned.

row.entry: Test strips, nano-fibrous films and cell imaging
Real sample analysis, Cell membrane imaging

head.ce:title: Synthesis of non-modified near-infrared carbon dots for hypochlorite detection and cell membrane imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Huanghuai University, Zhumadian 463000, Henan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Huanghuai University, Zhumadian 463000, Henan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of the NIR-CDs and their application for the detection of ClO− and cell membrane imaging.

Devising carbon dots with long wavelength emission (red light or near infrared), high selectivity and good bio-compatibility is critical in fluorescence detection and imaging, but achieving this goal remains a great challenge. Herein, near-infrared emissive carbon dots (NIR-CDs) with obvious emission characteristic of 653 nm were synthesized through hydrothermally treatment of toluidine bule and gallic acid. Noticeably, the NIR-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity to hypochlorite (ClO−), and the limit of detection is as low as 42.7 nM. The selective recognition reaction between ClO− and the surface functional groups of NIR-CDs inhibits the fluorescence from NIR-CDs. The quenching mechanism was confirmed by fluorescence lifetime decays, FT-IR spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectra. More remarkably, the NIR-CDs have rich hydrophilic groups showed lower cytotoxicity, excellent bio-compatibility and specific cell membrane localization ability. The established spectrofluorometric method based on NIR-CDs has been used to determination of ClO− level in tap water sample, the recoveries were 97.7 %–103.3 %. In addition, the NIR-CDs also has been successfully applied for the imaging of cell membrane. The study provides a novel idea for developing NIR ClO− probe as well as cell membrane localization probe based on CDs, which present bright prospects in real water samples monitoring and cell membrane imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging luminescent carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely concerned by researchers in recent decade [1,2]. Compared withconventional organic dyes, metal-doped nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are a promising alternative in the fields of fluorescence detection, cellular imaging and drug delivery due to their good water solubility, superior tunable fluorescence properties, environment friendliness and high biosecurity [3–5]. Furthermore, CDs with longer emission wavelength in near-infrared region (NIR-CDs, greater than 650 nm) are superior in terms of their advantages such as minimum auto-fluorescence interference, strong penetration ability, and weak photo-damage of biological tissue [6,7]. At present, researchers have made significant efforts in the development of NIR-CDs. The NIR-CDs with emission characteristics of 674 nm were prepared using albizia flowers as precursors via an ultrasonic-assisted method, and further used for monitoring Cu2+ in biosystems [8]. Through hydrothermally treatment of reduced glutathione and formamide, the NIR-CDs with an emission peak of 650 nm were obtained, and applied in the determination of cortisol [9]. A kind of up-conversion NIR-CDs with 784 nm emission characteristics were synthesized by stripping of red light CDs with microwave-assisted method, and further applied for vivo imaging [10]. However, owing to the fluorescence mechanism is still not clear, only a few reported CDs have successfully emitted NIR fluorescence, especially in the field of fluorescence detection. Therefore, the construction of NIR-CDs based fluorescent probes and further apply them to detect target molecules of interest in real samples still face a great challenge.

Nowadays, the normal life of human beings is seriously threatened by environmental problems, including the abuse of various substances and serious exceedance of the standards. Disinfection is an important link in the process of urban water treatment. The disinfectant used in this process can suppress diseases such as typhoid fever and cholera via strong oxidation of pathogenic microorganisms [11]. As an important active substance, hypochlorite (ClO−) possess strong oxidation capacity and is commonly used in sewage purification, water disinfection, household bleach, and many production processes related to our diets [12–14]. However, according to the WHO drinking water standard, the maximum permissible chlorine concentration is 5 mg·L−1 [15]. Long-term widespread use of ClO− may cause pollution and damage to air, groundwater and soil on which human survival depends [16]. Moreover, excess accumulation of ClO− may be a key factor leading to many human diseases, such as osteoarthritis, lung injury, kidney disease, and even tumor [17–19]. Accordingly, the development of simple, sensitive and rapid method to detect ClO− level in diverse sample matrix is of great significance for human safety and environmental protection.

Up till now, several fluorescence probes based on CDs have been investigated in ClO− determination. Recently, a kind of blue-green emissive CDs with 545 nm emission characteristics has been synthesized from 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid. The CDs can be used as colorimetric/fluorescent probe to detect ClO− and ascorbic acid in body fluid and cells [20]. By hydrothermally treatment of hyperbranched polyethyleneimine and 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid, the green emissive bifunctional carbon dots with emission at 545 nm have been prepared for detecting Cu2+ and ClO− in aqueous solution [21]. Through simply blending green emissive CDs and red emissive into one system, a ratiometric probe (I630/I525) has been prepared for sensing ClO− in living cells and zebrafish [22]. However, for all we know, the vast majority of the reported CDs-based ClO− probes emit short-wavelength blue-green fluorescence, which are not ideal candidates for further application in real samples. Moreover, some reported CDs are disturbed by metal ions in detecting ClO−, the specific selectivity of those CDs for ClO− needs to be further improved.

Taking the above situation into account, the NIR-CDs with 653 nm emission characteristics were synthesized via hydrothermal method using toluidine bule and gallic acid as raw materials. The NIR-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity to ClO−. In addition, NIR-CDs were able to locate on cell membrane without any modification. Cell membrane is an amphiphilic bilayer membrane composed of lipids and proteins, which is closely related to signal transduction in the extracellular environment and can induce further responses by changes in morphology and shape [23]. As a promising NIR emitting nanomaterial, the NIR-CDs were successfully applied as a bi-functional fluorescent probe for the determination of ClO− level in water samples and the imaging of cell membrane (Scheme 1 ).
The details are provided in supplementary material.

9.2 mg of toluidine blue and 5.1 mg of gallic acid were dispersed in 12 mL ultrapure water after ultrasonic treatment. The resulting solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and hydrothermal reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the collected suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min, the insoluble solids were removed, and then the supernatant was further filtered by microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Next, the resulting filtrate was poured into a dialysis bag (MW: 500 Da) and soaked in ultrapure water for 8 h to remove impurities such as raw materials and by-products. Finally, the collected supernatant was dried in a freeze-dryer to obtain solid-state NIR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedures of ClO− in solution
ce:section.ce:para: The NIR-CDs were prepared into a 5.0 mg·mL−1 aqueous solution for subsequent experiments. The determination of ClO− were performed in 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4). Typically, ClO− with different concentrations (0–25 μM) were respectively mixed with the NIR-CDs solution (15 μg·mL−1), and the final volume of the solution is constant at 4 mL. After incubated at 25 °C for 7 min, the excitation wavelength was set at 630 nm and the slit width was set at 5 nm/3 nm, and the corresponding emission spectra were measured. To investigate the selectivity of NIR-CDs to ClO−, the potential interfering substances including Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, S2−, SO3 2−, SCN−, S2O3 2−, H2PO4 −, Lys, Arg, GSH, H2O2, TBO•, 1O2, •OH, O2 −, ONOO−, TBHP and NO were mixed with NIR-CDs respectively, and the corresponding emission spectra were recorded. The above measurements were repeated three times.

The fresh tap water was collected from the laboratory to analyze ClO− content in real samples. To evaluate the accuracy of our method, we conducted standard additive recovery experiment, and the test method was consistent with the above.

The details of cytotoxicity assay are shown in supplementary material. MCF-7 cells were seeded in DMEM medium containing 1 % penicillin–streptomycin, and placed two groups of cell culture dishes in a cell incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, two groups of MCF-7 cells were incubated with DMEM containing NIR-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 3 h. After that, we rinsed one group of cells using PBS to remove the excess NIR-CDs, and then further co-incubated with ClO− (80 μM) for 20 min. Finally, the cells were rinsed twice with PBS, and the cell culture dishes were taken to confocal microscope for imaging analysis.

The TEM image in Fig. 1 A shows that the NIR-CDs are basically spherical and well dispersed. High-resolution HRTEM image (the small figure inserted in Fig. 1A) displays that the lattice structure of NIR-CDs is relatively clear, and the crystal spacing is measured at 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the (100) face of graphitic carbon [24,25]. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the size distribution range of the NIR-CDs is in 2.35–5.46 nm by statistics of the particle size of 116 particles, and the mean diameter is 3.35 ± 0.60 nm. XRD characterization result (Fig. 2 A) shows a wide diffraction peak appears at 2θ = 23.8°, which demonstrates that NIR-CDs have an amorphous carbon structure [26].

FT-IR characterization was conducted to analyze the surface functional groups of NIR-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the OH and NH bonds are confirmed by the characteristic peak at 3450 cm−1. The peak at 1601 cm−1 indicates the existence of CN and CO bonds. The peak of 1400 cm−1 corresponds to CN bond. In addition, the peak at 1221 cm−1 represents the presence of CO and CS. The above analysis results show that the NIR-CDs successfully inherited the functional groups of the precursors during the formation of particles.

The elemental composition of NIR-CDs was measured by elemental analysis. The results are presented in Table S1, the NIR-CDs mainly contains C, H, N, O and S, and the corresponding element contents are 24.37 %, 3.82 %, 9.37 %, 60.10 % and 2.34 %, respectively. In addition, the results of XPS measurements are presented in Fig. 3 . The full-scan spectrum of XPS (Fig. 3A) presents four major peaks at 284.7 eV, 532.0 eV, 399.2 eV and 164.2 eV, which are corresponded to C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p, respectively. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3B can be fitted to CO (288.7 eV), CO/CS (286.1 eV), CN (285.3 eV) and CC/CN (284.6 eV), respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) shows the presence of pyrrolic N (401.8 eV), pyridinic N (399.2 eV) and amino N (399.8 eV). The three peaks presented in S2p spectrum (Fig. 3D) show the presence of SO (169.1 eV), S2p1/2 (165.4 eV), and S2p3/2 (164.3 eV).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NIR-CDs were measured to explore their optical properties. Fig. 4 A depicts that the NIR-CDs have two strong absorption peaks at 287 nm and 625 nm. The π-π* transition of aromatic CC corresponds to the absorption peak at 287 nm, while the absorption peak at 625 nm is usually attributed to the excited state energy being captured by the surface state and can be used to produce strong fluorescence [27,28]. Fig. 4B presents that the position of emission peak of NIR-CDs is almost unaffected by the change of excitation wavelength (540 nm–650 nm), indicating that the emission wavelength of NIR-CDs is independent of excitation wavelength. In addition, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of NIR-CDs is 7.3 %.
The optical stability research of NIR-CDs was further conducted under different ionic strength, temperature, irradiation time and pH value. See supplementary materials for details.

To study the responsiveness of NIR-CDs to ClO−, the effects of various potential interferents including cations (e.g. Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+), anions (e.g. SO4 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, S2−, SO3 2−, SCN−, S2O3 2−, H2PO4 −), amino acids (e.g. Lys, Arg, GSH) and reactive species (e.g·H2O2, TBO•, 1O2, •OH, O2 −, ONOO−, TBHP, NO) on the fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs were measured under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5C and Fig. 5D, the fluorescence of NIR-CDs (15 μg·mL−1) is significantly quenched (Φ = 2.1 %) by ClO− (25 μM), and the quenching efficiency reaches 85 %. However, when other interfering ions (250 μM) are added into the NIR-CDs solution, the F/F0 has no significant change, indicating that the NIR-CDs is almost unresponsive to these analytes. The above results indicated the NIR-CDs have excellent selectivity to ClO−.

According to the reported literature [12,22,35], the aromatic amine groups on the surface of CDs can be oxidized by ClO− to produce non-fluorescent nitroso compounds, which is mostly accompanied by static fluorescence quenching mechanism. On this basis, the quenching mechanism of ClO− to NIR-CDs was investigated by fluorescence lifetime decays, FT-IR spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectra.

According to the measured fluorescence lifetime decay curve (Fig. S3A), the fluorescence lifetime of the NIR-CDs (15 μg·mL−1) changes from 0.89 ns to 0.82 ns after the addition of ClO− (12 μM), indicating that the presence of ClO− has little effect on the fluorescence lifetime of NIR-CDs, which means that the fluorescence quenching of NIR-CDs belong to a static quenching process [36,37]. In the FT-IR spectra of NIR-CDs (Fig. S3B), the characteristic peak at 3450 cm−1 represents the presence of the NH and OH bonds, indicating that the NIR-CDs successfully inherited the aromatic amine group and hydroxyl functional group of the precursors during particle formation. When NIR-CDs are mixed with ClO−, the stretching vibration peaks between 1590 cm−1 and 1310 cm−1 presented an obvious variations. This suggests that NIR-CDs may have been oxidized by ClO− [12]. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S3C) displays that after reacting with ClO−, the absorption peak intensity of the NIR-CDs at 287 nm and 625 nm is significantly weakened, and the absorption peak at 625 nm is red shifted. There is evidence [38] that this may be due to the changes in the surface functional groups of NIR-CDs after the reaction with ClO−. Therefore, we speculate that the most likely quenching mechanism is that the aromatic amine group on the surface of NIR-CDs is oxidized by ClO− and forms complexes with no fluorescence, which is accompanied by static quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of ClO− In tap water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The established spectrofluorometric method based on NIR-CDs was applied to the determination of ClO− in tap water. The results are presented in Table S2, the ClO− level in blank tap water is 2.69 μM. Furthermore, the level of ClO− in tap water could be detected sensitively with recoveries of 97.7–103.3 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are between 2.7 and 4.2 %. All above facts demonstrate that this method possess good reliability and stability for the determination of ClO− level in real water samples.

Prior to cell imaging studies, standard MTT assay was first carried out to assess the cytotoxicity of NIR-CDs. As can be seen from Fig. S4, the viability of MCF-7 cells is above 85 % after 20 h incubation with 0–400 μg·mL−1 NIR-CDs. In this study, 50 μg·mL−1 NIR-CDs was used to incubate MCF-7 cells, and the cells viability reached more than 98 %. The result demonstrates that at this experimental concentration, NIR-CDs have no marked cytotoxicity and are very suitable for the application in cell imaging experiments.

Cell imaging experiments in Fig. 6 B show that an intense red fluorescence can be seen in cell membrane region of MCF-7 cells after incubation using NIR-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 3 h. However, when the cells were further co-incubated with ClO− (80 μM) for 20 min, the fluorescence intensity of red channel is significantly reduced (Fig. 6D). Moreover, it can be noticed that the NIR-CDs does not penetrate the cell membrane into cytoplasmic region, indicating that NIR-CDs possess a specific cell membrane targeting ability. These results demonstrate that NIR-CDs can be used for bio-imaging ClO− and have a good application prospect in the aspects of cell membrane staining and cell membrane localization.

The cell membrane targeting capability of NIR-CDs was further verified by co-localization experiment with DiO (green channel, a commercially available cell membrane fluorescent marker). As depicted in Fig. 7 A–D, the green channel fluorescence signal of DiO matched well with the NIR channel fluorescence signal of NIR-CDs, and the calculated Pearson’s coefficient is 0.71 (Fig. 7E), indicating that the NIR-CDs can indeed be localized on the cell membrane.

To investigate the cell membrane localization function of NIR-CDs, the cell imaging studies on their precursor toluidine blue was carried out. Cell imaging results (Fig. S5) displays that after incubated cells using toluidine blue, the cell membrane region also shows bright fluorescence, indicating that toluidine blue can attach to cell membrane. According to the reported literatures [39–41], CDs can retain some fragments of precursors during the formation of particles, so that they can continue to maintain the properties of precursor molecules. Therefore, the cell membrane localization function of NIR-CDs may come from the property of precursor.

In this work, the NIR-CDs with low cytotoxicity, excellent water dispersibility and fluorescence stability were skillfully prepared with no complicated synthesis and surface modification. The fluorescence of NIR-CDs at 653 nm can be quenched by ClO− with high selectivity and low detection limit of 42.7 nM. Furthermore, the application of NIR-CDs to the detection of ClO− in tap water sample has obtained satisfactory results in terms of the relative standard deviation and recovery efficiency. Interestingly, the bio-imaging of MCF-7 cells was also achieved using the NIR-CDs, which showed the targeting capability on cell membrane. The NIR-CDs and the detection method developed in this work are simple, high-efficiency and low-cost, and have a good application prospect in the field of practical sample detection and cell membrane localization.

Lan-Fang Pang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wen-Le Fang: Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Bin Zhang: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Qi-Jie Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Programs for Science and Technology Development of Henan Province (242102230108 and 232102310360), and the National Project Cultivation Foundation of Huanghuai University (XKPY-2023007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124743.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
